---
title: "Rust Book Follow-Through"
author: "Badran Elshenawy"
date: 2026-03-18T16:19:00Z
format: html
---

# Context

-   This is my notes on the Rust book: https://rust-book.cs.brown.edu/

-   I have decided that this is the language I would like to learn to get an introduction to low-level programming and to enhance my tool-building capabilities

-   It does not seem easy at all but that's what makes it so interesting, and I have personally seen significant adoption in the field in the last two years

-   This leads me to believe that the tool has merits, and the appealing aspect to me is the introduction to low-level code that I never had any awareness of

-   As was the case for [R Packages](https://r-pkgs.org/), I am creating a Claude project to accompany this endeavor and help me create summary notes of the chapters I am going through

    -   I am also going to be version-controlling the entire experience, updating and pushing notes regularly

-   Exciting times ahead

# Introduction

Remembered! Here's the summary reformatted:

**What is Rust?**

-   Combines high-level ergonomics with low-level control
    -   Lets you manage memory without the usual hassle

**Who is Rust for?**

-   **Dev teams**
    -   Compiler catches subtle bugs before they ship
    -   Also catches concurrency bugs
    -   Tooling: Cargo (dependencies), Rustfmt (formatting), rust-analyzer (IDE support)
-   **Students**
    -   Great for learning systems concepts like OS development
    -   Welcoming community
-   **Companies**
    -   Used in production for CLI tools, web services, embedded devices, ML, browsers (Firefox), and more
-   **Open source contributors**
    -   Language, tools, and libraries are community-driven
-   **Speed & stability seekers**
    -   **Zero-cost abstractions:** safe code is also fast code

**Who is this book for?**

-   Assumes prior programming experience
    -   Not tied to any specific language
    -   Not suitable for complete beginners

**How to use the book**

-   Best read front-to-back

    -   Later chapters build on earlier ones

-   Two chapter types

    -   Concept chapters (theory)
    -   Project chapters (Chapters 2, 12, 21)

-   Key chapter highlights

    -   Ch 1: install & Hello World
    -   Ch 2: guessing game project
    -   Ch 4: ownership
    -   Ch 7: modules
    -   Ch 16: concurrency
    -   Ch 17: async/await
    -   Ch 21: multithreaded web server project

-   Reading compiler error messages is a core learning skill

-   **"Ferris"** the crab mascot flags problematic code:

    -   Won't compile
    -   Panics
    -   Misbehaves

**2026-03-18:** Introduction Complete

## Chapter 1: Getting Started

**Installation**

-   Install Rust via `rustup`

-   Verify Cargo with `cargo --version`

-   Pre-cache dependencies for offline work with `cargo add`

**Hello, World! (no Cargo)**

-   Source files use `.rs` extension

-   Compile directly with `rustc main.rs`

-   Every executable needs a `main` function

-   `println!` is a macro (note the `!`)

-   `rustc` is fine for trivial programs

    -   Cargo is better once complexity grows

**Cargo Commands**

-   `cargo new <name>`
    -   Creates project directory, `Cargo.toml`, and `src/main.rs`
    -   Initialises a Git repo by default
    -   Override with `--vcs` flag
-   `cargo init`
    -   Converts an existing folder into a Cargo project
-   `cargo build`
    -   Compiles to `target/debug/`
    -   Creates `Cargo.lock` to pin dependency versions
-   `cargo run`
    -   Builds and runs in one step
    -   Skips recompilation if source hasn't changed
-   `cargo check`
    -   Verifies code compiles without producing a binary
    -   Much faster than `cargo build`
    -   Ideal for frequent checks during development
-   `cargo build --release`
    -   Compiles with optimizations
    -   Output goes to `target/release/`
    -   Use for benchmarking and final distribution

**Project Structure**

-   `Cargo.toml`

    -   Uses TOML format
    -   `[package]` section: name, version, edition
    -   `[dependencies]` section: list crates here

-   `src/` holds all source code

-   Top-level directory is for READMEs, licenses, config

**Typical Workflow (existing project)**

-   `git clone <url>`

-   `cd <project>`

-   `cargo build`

**TOML Format**

-   Stands for "Tom's Obvious, Minimal Language"

-   A configuration file format designed to be easy to read

-   Key-value pairs: `name = "hello_cargo"`

-   Section headers in square brackets: `[package]`, `[dependencies]`

-   Supports strings, integers, floats, booleans, arrays, and tables

-   Intentionally minimal

    -   No deeply nested structures
    -   Designed to map unambiguously to a hash table

-   A typical `Cargo.toml` looks like:

``` toml
[package]
name = "hello_cargo"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2024"

[dependencies]
rand = "0.8.5"
```

**Parallels with R Packages**

-   The structural similarity to R packages is striking

-   Nearly every design choice in Cargo has a direct analogue in R's package system

-   `Cargo.toml` ↔ `DESCRIPTION`

    -   Both are the single metadata file that defines the package
    -   Both declare name, version, and dependencies

-   `src/` ↔ `R/`

    -   Source code lives in a dedicated subdirectory
    -   Top-level directory is reserved for config and documentation

-   `Cargo.lock` ↔ `renv.lock`

    -   Both pin exact dependency versions for reproducibility
    -   Both are auto-generated and not meant to be hand-edited

-   `cargo build` ↔ `R CMD build` / `devtools::build()`

-   `cargo check` ↔ `R CMD check` / `devtools::check()`

    -   Both verify correctness without producing a final distributable

-   `cargo test` ↔ `devtools::test()`

    -   Both run the project's test suite from one command

-   `cargo new` ↔ `usethis::create_package()`

    -   Both scaffold a new project with the conventional directory layout

-   Crates (from crates.io) ↔ Packages (from CRAN)

    -   Centralised repository of community packages
    -   Versioned dependencies resolved automatically

-   `cargo add` ↔ `usethis::use_package()`

    -   Both add a dependency to the metadata file without manual editing

-   The core philosophy is the same: convention over configuration

    -   Both ecosystems enforce an opinionated project structure
    -   Tooling automates building, checking, testing, and distributing
    -   If you're comfortable with R's package conventions, Cargo should feel very familiar

**2026-03-23:** Deep focus here

-   Finished all of chapter 1 in a single sitting

    -   In the age of agentic AI, multi-tasking seems like a great thing to do but I prefer linear workflows with deep focus at every juncture

        -   That does not mean spending the whole day on one task but rather going through 10 meaningful tasks one step at a time, only moving to the next task when the first is completely finished

    -   The reason I say this is that I have realized that the energy burnt through context switching is far more severe than the energy burnt by the tasks themselves

        -   Multi-tasking seems cool and sexy, but deep work is the way to go

        -   It's good to build micro-habits to ensure continual progress, and this works well with, not against, a deep work approach where valuable projects are given sufficient undivided attention for extended durations of time

## Chapter 2: Programming a Guessing Game

-   Variables are **immutable by default** — add `mut` to opt into mutability

    -   Unusual compared to Python/R where everything is mutable
    -   Immutability is a deliberate safety feature

-   `use std::io` brings modules into scope

    -   Very similar to Python's `import`

-   `::` denotes associated functions or module paths (e.g. `String::new()`, `std::io::stdin()`)

    -   Similar to R's namespacing (e.g. `dplyr::filter()`)

-   Associated functions like `String::new()` are functions implemented on a type

    -   Similar to Python class methods like `dict.fromkeys()` or constructors like `str()`
    -   `new` is a common convention but not special syntax — just a naming pattern

-   References (`&`) let multiple parts of code access data without copying

    -   Also **immutable by default** — use `&mut` for mutable references
    -   No direct equivalent in Python/R, where references are handled implicitly

-   `Result` enums (`Ok`/`Err`) are Rust's standard error-handling pattern

    -   The compiler actively warns you if you ignore a `Result`
    -   `.expect("message")` is a quick way to crash on error

-   `println!` uses `{}` as placeholders

    -   `{variable_name}` works like Python f-strings but without the `f` prefix
    -   Empty `{}` with trailing arguments works like `.format()`

-   `//` for line comments (same as R, vs. `#` in Python)

-   `fn main() {}` — function syntax is a mix of Python and R conventions

    -   Uses `{}` for blocks like R's `function() {}`

**2026-03-26:** Stopped at [Generating a Secret Number](https://rust-book.cs.brown.edu/ch02-00-guessing-game-tutorial.html#generating-a-secret-number)

-   Good and quick progress here, and I finally managed to turn off code auto-completion in vs code so I can type this out myself

-   Loving this language thusfar despite its finnicky aspects, but I do not think I mind any of it

**Adding Dependencies**

-   Rust's standard library doesn't include random number generation, so you need the external `rand` crate

-   Add dependencies in `Cargo.toml` under the `[dependencies]` section (e.g., `rand = "0.8.5"`)

-   Version specifiers follow **Semantic Versioning (SemVer)**

    -   `0.8.5` is shorthand for `^0.8.5`
    -   This means any version ≥ 0.8.5 but \< 0.9.0

**Building with Dependencies**

-   Running `cargo build` with a new dependency triggers several steps
    -   Updates the `crates.io` registry index
    -   Downloads the specified crate and all its transitive dependencies
    -   Compiles everything
-   Cargo is smart about rebuilding
    -   If nothing changes, it does nothing
    -   If only your code changes, it recompiles just your crate

**Reproducible Builds & Cargo.lock**

-   The `Cargo.lock` file ensures reproducible builds
    -   Created on first build, records exact dependency versions
    -   Future builds use these locked versions rather than resolving again
    -   Should be checked into source control
        -   very smart convention here, and R is starting to adopt something similar with `renv.lock`

**Updating Dependencies**

-   `cargo update` refreshes dependencies within your specified range
    -   Ignores `Cargo.lock` and resolves the latest compatible versions
    -   Still respects the version bounds in `Cargo.toml`
        -   This means that it will get the latest 0.8.x version but will not jump to version 0.9.x where there could an API change that would break the code
            -   The only way to jump to that later minor release or another major release entirely is to specify it in the `cargo.toml`
                -   This book is much easier to go through now that I understand package development conventions a lot more from my work with R packages
    -   To jump to a new major/minor version (e.g., 0.9.x), you must manually edit `Cargo.toml`

**2026-03-27:** Stopped at [Generating a Random Number](https://rust-book.cs.brown.edu/ch02-00-guessing-game-tutorial.html#generating-a-random-number)

-   Small progress but consistent and that is key

    -   In the midst of everything I am doing, consistency is becoming harder sadly

**Generating a random number**

-   `use rand::Rng;` brings the `Rng` trait into scope

    -   Traits define methods that types implement

    -   The trait must be in scope to call its methods

-   `rand::thread_rng()` returns a random number generator local to the current thread, seeded by the OS

-   `.gen_range(1..=100)` generates a number in an inclusive range

    -   `start..=end` syntax means both bounds are included

-   `cargo doc --open` builds and opens docs for all your dependencies locally

    -   Running `cargo doc --open` is so cool because it builds the vignettes for the packages you are using, opens that in an HTML in your browser, and allows to navigate to your desired package and function documentation

**Comparing the guess to the secret number**

-   `use std::cmp::Ordering;` brings in an enum with three variants: `Less`, `Greater`, `Equal`

-   `.cmp(&other)` compares two values and returns an `Ordering` variant

    -   Takes a reference to the value you're comparing against

-   `match` expression checks the result against each "arm"

    -   Each arm has a pattern and code to run if the pattern matches

    -   Stops after the first successful match

    -   Similar to Python's `match`/`case` (structural pattern matching)

**Type mismatch and conversion**

-   `guess` is a `String`, `secret_number` defaults to `i32`

    -   Rust won't compare a string to a number

-   Fix: shadow `guess` with a converted version

    -   `let guess: u32 = guess.trim().parse().expect("Please type a number!");`

-   `.trim()` strips whitespace and newline characters (`\n` or `\r\n`)

-   `.parse()` converts a string to another type

    -   The target type is inferred from the `u32` annotation

-   Annotating `guess` as `u32` also causes Rust to infer `secret_number` as `u32`

    -   Type inference flows both directions

**Shadowing**

-   Rust lets you declare a new variable with the same name as an existing one

-   The new binding "shadows" the old one

-   Common use case: converting a value from one type to another while reusing the name

**Looping for multiple guesses**

-   `loop { }` creates an infinite loop

    -   Like Python's `while True:`

-   The guess-input and comparison logic moves inside the loop

-   Without a `break`, the game keeps asking forever

**Quitting after a correct guess**

-   Add `break` inside the `Ordering::Equal` arm

    -   Uses a block `{ }` to run multiple statements in one arm

-   Exiting the loop exits the program, since the loop is the last thing in `main`

**Handling invalid input gracefully**

-   Replace `.expect(...)` on `.parse()` with a `match` expression

    -   `Ok(num) => num` extracts the parsed number on success

    -   `Err(_) => continue` skips to the next loop iteration on failure

-   `_` is a catch-all pattern that matches any value

-   `continue` jumps back to the top of the loop

-   This is a shift from "crash on error" to "handle the error"

    -   `expect` = crash with a message

    -   `match` on `Result` = inspect `Ok` / `Err` and decide what to do

**2026-03-31:** Chapter 2 complete

-   Rapid progress here

    -   This programming language is finnicky, gets annoyed at the slightest mismatches, and I absolutely love it

        -   This is the kind of language that needs you to clearly tell it that the type of variable (string, number, float, whatever), and I absolutely love that

            -   After working with high-level languages all of my life, I find the raw specificity of low-level coding languages to be rather fascinating

-   I am also trailing something new here

    -   I am not going through the notes now, but I will go through them during my next session to refresh my memory about the previous one and to keep momentum

## Chapter 3: Common Programming Concepts

**Variables and Mutability**

-   Variables are immutable by default — the opposite of Python and R, where reassignment is always allowed
    -   `let x = 5;` — x cannot be reassigned after this
-   You can add `mut` to allow reassignment, but this is generally poor practice
    -   `let mut x = 5;` then `x = 6;` — works but discouraged
    -   It opens the door to bugs where one part of code assumes a value is stable while another changes it
    -   Prefer creating a new variable or using shadowing instead

**Shadowing**

-   Using `let` again with the same name creates a completely new variable
    -   `let x = 5;` then `let x = x + 1;` — x is now 6, but via a new variable
    -   The old one is hidden, not modified
    -   This is the idiomatic alternative to `mut` when you need to transform a value
-   Unlike `mut`, shadowing allows changing the type
    -   `let spaces = "   ";` then `let spaces = spaces.len();` — string to number
-   Scoped to blocks:
    -   A shadow inside `{}` disappears when the block ends
    -   The outer variable is restored

**Constants**

-   Declared with `const` and can live in the global scope

    -   `const MAX_SCORE: u32 = 100;`
    -   Unlike `let`, which can only exist inside functions
    -   Similar to defining a constant at the top of an R or Python script, but enforced by the compiler

-   Must have a type annotation and can only be set to compile-time expressions

-   Convention: `ALL_UPPERCASE_WITH_UNDERSCORES`

**2026-04-04:** Stopped at section 3.2

-   Quick progress here but not very substantial

    -   Quiet happy with it still and look forward to learning about data types soon

**Data Types**

-   Rust is statically typed — all types must be known at compile time
    -   Like R and Python, the compiler can usually infer types from context
    -   When it can't, you must annotate: `let guess: u32 = "42".parse().expect("Not a number!");`

**Scalar Types**

-   Rust has four: integers, floats, booleans, and characters
    -   Same basic categories as Python and R, but with explicit size control

**Integers**

-   Come in signed (`i`) and unsigned (`u`) variants at fixed bit widths
    -   `i32` is the default — signed, 32-bit
    -   `u8` holds 0–255, `i8` holds -128–127, and so on up to 128-bit
-   `isize` and `usize` match your system architecture (usually 64-bit)
    -   Mainly used for indexing collections
-   You can use `_` as a visual separator
    -   `1_000` is the same as `1000` — like Python's `1_000`
-   Number literals can carry a type suffix
    -   `57u8` explicitly makes it a `u8`

**Integer Overflow**

-   Rust protects against overflow, unlike Python (which auto-expands) or R (which silently coerces)
    -   In debug mode: the program panics (crashes with an error)
    -   In release mode: values wrap around (e.g. 256 becomes 0 for `u8`)
    -   Relying on wrapping is considered a bug
-   The standard library provides explicit overflow-handling methods
    -   `wrapping_add` — always wraps
    -   `checked_add` — returns `None` on overflow
    -   `saturating_add` — clamps at the min/max value

**2026-04-06:** Stopped at [Floating-Point Types](https://rust-book.cs.brown.edu/ch03-02-data-types.html#floating-point-types)

-   Small but consistent always wins the race

-   I finally understood why u8 only represents 0-255 integers and the difference between `u` and `i` in `u16` vs `i16`

    -   The trick here is that the first bit corresponds to 128, second to 64, third to 32, etc.

        -   For u16, the first bit is 32768 and so on

**Floating-Point Types**

-   Two types: `f32` (32-bit) and `f64` (64-bit, the default)
    -   `let x = 2.0;` — defaults to `f64`
    -   `let y: f32 = 3.0;` — must annotate for `f32`
-   Default is `f64` because it's roughly the same speed as `f32` on modern CPUs but more precise
    -   R also defaults to 64-bit floats; Python's `float` is 64-bit too

**Numeric Operations**

-   Standard operators: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%`
    -   Same as Python and R
-   Integer division truncates toward zero
    -   `-5 / 3` gives `-1`, not `-2`
    -   Python floors instead (`-5 // 3` gives `-2`), so this is a subtle difference

**Booleans**

-   `true` and `false` — lowercase, like Python
    -   R uses uppercase `TRUE` / `FALSE`
    -   `let f: bool = false;`

**Characters**

-   `char` uses single quotes, strings use double quotes
    -   `let c = 'z';`
    -   Like R's distinction, but enforced — Python treats both the same
-   A `char` is 4 bytes and represents a full Unicode scalar value
    -   Includes emoji, CJK characters, accented letters
    -   Not the same as a "character" in the everyday sense — more on this in Ch. 8
    -   This means that every single letter is represented by 32 bits
        -   Since 8 bits is 1 byte, each character is represented by 4 bytes/32 bits to enclose all of the potential characters in unicode
            -   It's all 1s and 0s after all
            -   There is also a subtle distinction here between `''` and `""` where the first is a character and the latter is a string
                -   In both R and python, characters and strings are the same thing, but it seems like they mean different things here in rust
                    -   more on this later apparently in chapter 8

**2026-04-07:** Stopped at [Compound Types](https://rust-book.cs.brown.edu/ch03-02-data-types.html#compound-types)

-   Short and consistent

**Tuples**

-   Group multiple values of different types into one compound type

    -   `let tup: (i32, f64, u8) = (500, 6.4, 1);`
    -   Similar to Python tuples, but with fixed types per position

-   **Fixed length** — cannot grow or shrink

    -   This is very different from python where tuples can have variable lengths and can have elements added or removed

-   Two ways to access values:

    -   Destructuring: `let (x, y, z) = tup;` — like Python's unpacking
    -   Dot indexing: `tup.0`, `tup.1`, `tup.2` — unique to Rust, not bracket-based

-   An empty tuple `()` is called the **unit** type

    -   It's Rust's version of "returns nothing" — similar to Python's `None` or R's `NULL`

**Arrays**

-   Fixed-length collection where all elements **must be the same type**

    -   `let a = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];`
    -   Unlike Python lists or R vectors, they cannot grow or shrink

-   Bracket indexing: `a[0]`, `a[1]` — standard syntax like Python and R

-   Type annotation includes the length: `let a: [i32; 5]`

    -   The first entry in the type array is the type of the elements in the array (signed integer 32 in this case), and the second is the length of the array

-   Shorthand to fill with one value: `let a = [3; 5];` gives five 3s

-   If you need a resizable collection, use a `Vec` instead (covered in Ch. 8)

    -   This is the closer equivalent to Python's `list` or R's `c()`

**Indexing syntax difference**

-   Tuples use **dot notation**: `tup.0`

-   Arrays use **bracket notation**: `arr[0]`

-   Both are zero-indexed

**Bounds checking**

-   Accessing an out-of-bounds array index causes a panic at runtime
    -   Rust refuses to allow invalid memory access
    -   In C/C++, this would silently read garbage memory
    -   Python and R raise errors too, but Rust's check is a core safety guarantee
-   This is called a `runtime error` because the code does not error during compilation (code syntax is correct), but it does fail because the user inputted an inaccessible value
    -   This kind of error can only be detected at runtime after the code has been written and compiled, hence the name

**2026-04-09:** Stopped at section 3.3. [Functions](https://rust-book.cs.brown.edu/ch03-03-how-functions-work.html#functions)

-   Good progress here and we are done with this section on data types

    -   Lots of core CS concepts that I am learning as I go through this language, so quite happy with progress even if it is not rapid

Good catch — here's the corrected order:

**Functions**

-   Declared with `fn`, followed by the name, parentheses, and curly-brace body
    -   `fn another_function() { ... }`
-   Rust uses **snake_case** for function and variable names
    -   Same convention as Python
    -   R is looser — uses dots (`read.csv`), camelCase, or snake_case depending on the package
-   **Order doesn't matter** — you can define a function after or before it's called
    -   Unlike Python, where a function must be defined before the call executes
    -   R behaves similarly to Rust here (functions just need to exist by the time they're called)
-   **Parameters require explicit type annotations**
    -   `fn f(x: i32, unit: char) { ... }`
    -   Python doesn't require them (though type hints exist); R has no type annotations at all
    -   This is a deliberate Rust design choice — it lets the compiler infer types almost everywhere else
        -   This is a choice that I strongly agreed with because it is a lot of work upfront, but it makes debugging so much easier—they do make the case for this design choice in the book, and I completely agree with it

**Statements vs. Expressions**

-   **Statements** perform an action and return no value
    -   `let y = 6;` is a statement
    -   Function definitions are statements
-   **Expressions** evaluate to a value
    -   `5 + 6`, function calls, macro calls are all expressions
    -   A curly-brace block `{ ... }` is also an expression (and a syntactic scope)
        -   `let y = { let x = 3; x + 1 };` → `y` is `4`
-   **The semicolon is the switch**: adding `;` to an expression turns it into a statement (returns nothing)
    -   This is unique to Rust — Python and R have no equivalent distinction
-   **Unlike C/Ruby**, assignment (`let y = 6`) does not return a value
    -   So `let x = (let y = 6);` won't compile
    -   In Python, `:=` (walrus operator) does return a value; in R, `<-` inside `()` also returns

**Functions with Return Values**

-   Return type declared with `->` after the parameter list
    -   `fn five() -> i32 { 5 }`
-   The **last expression** (no semicolon) is implicitly returned
    -   No `return` keyword needed (though `return` exists for early exits)
    -   Python and R both require explicit `return` (or R implicitly returns the last evaluated expression — similar concept)
-   Accidentally adding `;` to the last line causes a **mismatched types** error
    -   The function returns `()` (unit type) instead of the declared type

**Comments**

-   Single-line: `//`

    -   Same as R's `#` purpose, different syntax
    -   Python uses `#`

-   Multi-line: `/* ... */`

    -   Python and R have no native multi-line comment syntax (Python uses `"""` docstrings as a workaround)

-   Can go above a line, at the end of a line, or span multiple lines with `//` on each

-   **Documentation comments** (`///`) exist too — covered later in Ch. 14

**`if` Expressions**

-   Basic syntax: `if condition { ... } else { ... }`
    -   No parentheses around the condition (unlike C/Java)
    -   Python also skips parens; R requires them
-   The condition **must** be a `bool` — no truthy/falsy coercion
    -   `if number { ... }` won't compile; you need `if number != 0 { ... }`
    -   Python and R both do implicit truthiness checks (`if x:` / `if (x)`)
-   `else if` chains work as expected
    -   Only the **first** true branch executes (same as Python's `elif` / R's `else if`)
    -   For many branches, Rust recommends `match` (covered in Ch. 6)
-   `if` is an **expression**, not just a statement
    -   You can assign its result: `let y = if cond { 1 } else { 2 };`
    -   Python equivalent: ternary `y = 1 if cond else 2`
    -   R equivalent: `y <- if (cond) 1 else 2`

**2026-04-17:** Stopped at section 3.5: [Using `if` in a `let` Statement](https://rust-book.cs.brown.edu/ch03-05-control-flow.html#using-if-in-a-let-statement)

-   I have dropped these comments for quite a bit, and I think it's a horrid idea they are vital for my work

-   I am not dropping them again despite the extra bit it adds for me to commit them and work with them

    -   I would much rather have something that grounds me in what I know than just go through the code with zero documentation

**Using `if` in a `let` Statement**

-   Because `if` is an expression, you can assign its result directly
    -   `let number = if condition { 5 } else { 6 };`
-   **Both arms must return the same type**
    -   `if condition { 5 } else { "six" }` won't compile — `i32` vs `&str`
    -   Rust needs to know the type at compile time, not runtime
    -   Python and R are dynamically typed, so `x = 5 if cond else "six"` is perfectly fine

**Loops**

-   Rust has three loop types: `loop`, `while`, `for`

-   **`loop`** — runs forever until explicitly stopped

    -   `loop { println!("again!"); }`
    -   No direct Python/R equivalent
        -   Closest is `while True:` in Python or `while (TRUE)` in R

-   **`break`** exits the current loop; **`continue`** skips to the next iteration

    -   Same keywords and behavior as Python
    -   R uses `break` and `next` instead of `continue`

**Returning Values from Loops**

-   `loop` is an expression — you can return a value via `break`
    -   `let result = loop { ... break counter * 2; };`
    -   The value after `break` becomes the loop's result
-   This is unique to Rust — Python and R loops can't return values directly
    -   In Python/R you'd assign inside the loop body instead
-   `break` vs `return`:
    -   `break` exits the current loop
    -   `return` exits the entire function
-   Both `break` and `return` are treated as having type `()` (unit) since code after them never executes

**2026-04-23:** Stopped at [Loop Labels to Disambiguate Between Multiple Loops](https://rust-book.cs.brown.edu/ch03-05-control-flow.html#loop-labels-to-disambiguate-between-multiple-loops)

-   Not great progress but good to be back

    -   Code is not horrid to read but details are complex
